title: Photon blockade via three-body interactions: toward high-purity and bright single-photon sources
abstract: Photon blockade is vital for single-photon generation, but current schemes with conventional and unconventional photon blockade face critical limitations like the purity-brightness trade-off, hindering the generation of high-performance single-photons. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a fundamentally new photon blockade mechanism by utilizing three-body interactions between a single photonic mode and two qubits. This kind of interaction intrinsically cuts off the excitation path to the two-photon state, resulting in a perfect photon blockade effect. The mechanism operates across a broad parameter range, free from the constraints of strong coupling or weak driving. Remarkably, it breaks the purity-brightness trade-off, enabling the simultaneous achievement of extreme purity and high brightness, both significantly outperforming previous mechanisms. Furthermore, this approach demonstrates robustness against thermal noise and avoids unwanted oscillations in the time-delayed correlation function. This work provides a path for generating high-purity, high-brightness, and robust single-photon sources, a key resource for quantum technologies.
summary: This paper introduces a photon blockade mechanism based on three-body interactions between a bosonic mode and two qubits. This approach overcomes the traditional trade-off between photon purity and brightness, providing a robust method for generating high-quality single photons. The mechanism is applicable across various platforms, including superconducting circuits, magnonics, and optomechanics.
main_problem: Existing photon blockade mechanisms, such as conventional and unconventional photon blockade, suffer from a fundamental trade-off where increasing brightness leads to decreased single-photon purity.
main_result: The authors propose a new photon blockade mechanism using three-body interactions that breaks the purity-brightness trade-off, enabling simultaneous high purity and high brightness while remaining robust against thermal noise.
method: The study uses the Lindblad master equation to model system dynamics and employs the QuTiP package for numerical steady-state simulations, supplemented by analytical solutions in the two-photon subspace.
model_or_system: A hybrid quantum system consisting of a single bosonic mode (photonic, magnonic, or phononic) coupled to two qubits via a three-body interaction term $J(a^\dagger \sigma^+_1 \sigma^-_2 + a \sigma^-_1 \sigma^+_2)$.
key_observables: Second-order correlation function $g^{(2)}(0)$ (purity) and average photon number $N$ (brightness).
important_parameters: Coupling strength $J$ relative to decay rate $\kappa$, driving Rabi frequency $\Omega$, detuning $\Delta$, and thermal occupation $n_{th}$.
main_assumptions: The mechanism is presented within the context of a microwave photonic platform, though the authors assert its universality to other bosonic modes.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows color maps of $g^{(2)}(0)$ and plots of $\log_{10} g^{(2)}(0)$ and $\log_{10} N$ as functions of detuning and coupling strength.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the purity-brightness trade-off, presents the three-body interaction model, provides analytical and numerical results demonstrating the breaking of the trade-off, discusses robustness against noise and driving strength, and concludes with applications to microwave platforms.
why_interesting: It provides a novel way to bypass fundamental limits in quantum optics and circuit QED, offering a path toward high-performance single-photon sources that are robust to experimental imperfections like thermal noise and strong driving.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The mechanism of CPB originated from energy-level anharmonicity. (b) Novel mechanism of PB based on three-body in- teraction, where no transition path exists between single-photon and two-photon states.

FIG. 2. (a) Color map of log10 g(2)(0) versus normalized coupling J/κ and detuning ∆/κ. The dashed line marks the optimal detuning trajectory, corresponding to the minimal g(2)(0) at each fixed J/κ. (b) Variations of log10 g(2)(0) and log10 N with ∆/κ for different coupling strengths J. For (a) and (b), we take Ω= 0.1κ and γ = 0.1κ. (c, d) g(2)(0) and N versus Ωfor different (c) J (at γ = 0.1κ) and (d) qubit dissipation rates γ. Solid and open circles denote the rising inflection points of g(2)(0) and the saturation points of N, respectively. (e, f) Color maps of (e) log10 g(2)(0) and (f) log10 N in the Ω-γ parameter space at J = 0.1κ and ∆= 0. The black and white dashed contours indicate...

FIG. 3. Performance comparison among TPB, CPB, and UPB. Scaled photon emission rate S/J and (b) log10 g(2)(0) versus log10(κ/J). (c) Time evolution of the time-delayed second-order correlation function log10 g(2)(t). (d) log10 g(2)(0) as a function of the thermal occupation number nth. Optimized JC-model parame- ters for CPB and UPB are detailed in the Supplemental Material [68].
